item.title: Stop overpaying for home lab hardware when this mini PC is $310 off
item.itunes:title: Stop overpaying for home lab hardware when this mini PC is $310 off
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
It's expensive to put together a new PC nowadays, and that impact is felt even more when the hardware isn't going to be part of your primary setup. However, if you're someone that's been intrigued about home labs, a new mini PC might be a great option to get things started at an affordable price.

This model from KAMRUI packs everything you need, despite coming in at an extremely compact size. When it comes to the performance, you're looking at pretty respectable power here thanks to the Intel Core i5-14450HX chip that's paired with 32GB of RAM and 1TB SSD. The best part is that you can also upgrade the RAM and storage, maxing out at 64GB of RAM and 4TB of SSD storage.

Something new for your home lab

But what makes this one truly special is the price. While it normally retails for $820 on Amazon, it can now be had for much less, coming in at $510 for a very limited time. That's $310 in savings from the MSRP, which makes it an absolute steal if you've been looking for a powerful mini PC that can do it all.

Naturally, mini PCs can handle a ton of different scenarios, replacing a full-size desktop for everyday use, or just becoming part of your home lab. You can also make it the dedicated hub of your entertainment center, security system, or use it as a storage solution. No matter what you want to do, this model will be able to handle it.

When it comes to port selection, you're getting quite a bit here, with two USB-A and one USB-C on the front. The rear panel has HDMI, DisplayPort, Ethernet, and four USB-A ports. There's also wireless connectivity with Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. Just be sure to grab this model while it's on sale because this deal won't last long.

KAMRUI Hyper H2 Mini PC
$510 $820 Save $310
CPU
Intel Core 14450HX
Memory
32GB DDR4
Storage
1TB NVMe SSD

A versatile mini PC that's also on sale, with a discount that knocks $310 off. Get it now for its lowest price and save big.

$510 at Amazon

item.title: Stop overpaying for mechanical keyboards — this Keychron deal proves you don't have to
item.itunes:title: Stop overpaying for mechanical keyboards — this Keychron deal proves you don't have to
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Mechanical keyboards aren't just for gamers, and can be a real upgrade for anyone that's hammering out hours of text every day. And while mechanical keyboards used to be pretty costly, some of the best options are now quite affordable.

While there are plenty of brands to choose from, Keychron is a pretty safe bet for veterans and newcomers alike. With that said, the Keychron K10 mechanical keyboard is now on sale, delivering a great experience at a fair price. While it's normally $70, it can now be had at its lowest price ever at $59.

What's great about the Keychron K10 mechanical keyboard?

Despite its simple look, this keyboard provides everything you need, without breaking the bank. You get a total of 104 keys, which includes a number pad, and white backlit keys, along with wired and wireless operation. The switches are mechanical and can be swapped out when needed, with this model coming with Keychron's Gateron G Pro Red Switches.

These are linear switches that deliver a responsive feel yet are extremely quiet, which makes them great for everyday use. Keychron shares that this switch is great for general typing and gaming. In addition to the above, this keyboard is compatible with a wide range of devices, which makes it incredibly versatile.

Unlock Mechanical Keyboard Deals—Subscribe to Newsletter

Subscribe to the newsletter for curated mechanical-keyboard coverage: deals, hands-on reviews, switch and mod guides, and practical buying tips to help you pick, customize, and maintain the right board.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

When it comes to battery life, you're looking at up to 240 hours of use, and charging via USB-C. There's also a two-level adjustment, which can help improve the typing experience. For the most part, you can't go wrong here with this model, especially at its new price. Get it now while you still can.

Keychron K10
$59 $70 Save $11

Proficient typists are probably familiar with mechanical keyboards, and the Keychron K10 is a great one for productivity. It has a full-size layout, classic design, and it gives you three types of Gateron G Pro switches to choose from.

$59 at Amazon

item.title: Google finally lets you change your Gmail address, but there's a catch
item.itunes:title: Google finally lets you change your Gmail address, but there's a catch
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Google now lets US users change Gmail handles and still receive mail at old and new addresses.
  • The feature is available in the US only for now; international users must wait.
  • There's a 12-month cooldown after changing; pick your new address carefully and avoid typos.

The big problem with online services requiring an email address to sign in is that you end up needing to create one while you're young. This means you'll likely end up creating an email address that sounded cool in the moment, but didn't age so well a decade later. At that point, you have to choose whether you'll cut ties with your embarrassing handle or forge ahead and hold true to your younger self's vision.

Well, it turns out that plenty of people have created Gmail handles they later regret, to the point that Google has released a new option to change them. And while changing your handle seems pretty painless, there is one major caveat you'll need to keep in mind

I tried Outlook but here's why I'm sticking with Gmail

Ultimately Gmail wins out as the best email client for me

1
By  Megan Ellis

Google now allows people to change their Gmail handles in the US only

Let's hope it becomes an international thing

Over on Google's blog The Keyword, the company officially announced that people can now change their Gmail address handles. You can tweak your own by going to your Google Account settings, then clicking Personal Info, then Email, then Google Account Email. If you're eligible for a name change, you should see a big blue "Change Google Account email" button. Hit that, and you'll have the option to change your handle.

The bad news is that this feature is only available in the US, so people in overseas countries may need to wait a little longer before they can finally exorcise the Gmail address of their 11-year-old self. The good news is that, if you do qualify for a name change, Google will let you receive emails via your old and new addresses to the same inbox. That means you get all of the benefits of a name change without needing to go through all your online accounts and update your email address.

People in the US should have the option to change their email starting today, but make sure you're truly settled on the address you want before you lock it in. Google says that there's a 12-month cooldown for changing your address, so any typos that sneak in will need to hang around for a year before you get the chance to fix them.

I moved my entire photo library off Google Photos — it cost more than I expected

I don't know if I regret it.

30
By  Jasmine Mannan

item.title: Your old Android phone has a sensor suite that most IoT devices can only dream of
item.itunes:title: Your old Android phone has a sensor suite that most IoT devices can only dream of
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
It's easy to spend $30 on a single-purpose Zigbee motion sensor or even $60 on smart air quality monitors, while a far more powerful device just sits in your junk drawer. A standard ESP32-based IoT sensor node requires soldering, a case, a power supply, and even lacks a screen, but your old phone has all of that, plus a high-resolution camera and a dual-core or sometimes even better processor.

It even has its own built-in battery, so unlike dedicated IoT sensors that die when the power blinks, a phone can keep logging data and sending alerts even during a blackout. While most people see an old Galaxy or Pixel as e-waste, power users see a high-performance Linux-based computer with 100+ worth of integrated sensors and a built-in battery. Don't just leave it to sit in a drawer collecting dust.

I finally set up Home Assistant with Zigbee, and my entire home changed

I had heard about Zigbee for a long time, but I finally took the plunge. I should have sooner.

11
By  Adam Conway

What sensors are present on your phone?

You can put them all to good use

There are so many sensors standard on phones, but if you want to invest in the same sensors for your home, they're premium in the IoT world. An IMU gyro sensor comes in the majority of phones and is standard. You also get an ambient light sensor, a microphone, a barometer, a magnetometer, and even a camera.

All of these would need to be purchased separately when picking up IoT components, and the majority of the time, the sensors present in old phones are better quality overall. An ambient light sensor in a phone is highly sensitive and can distinguish between just dim and sunset. Microphones can be used as a glass break detector or a noise floor monitor. A magnetometer can detect if a large metal object, like a car or garage door, is present. The camera on your old smartphone is likely 12MP plus resolution versus the grainy 240p of cheap IOT cameras.

Using your old phone as even just one of these sensors, let alone all of them, provides you with a much better purpose for your old device than just having it sit on a shelf. It can save you from investing in lower-quality IoT sensors, which can cost you a whole lot of money when all the components are added up.

However, there is a software bridge. The hardware itself is useless without a way to get the data out. There is a range of no-code or low-coding tools you can use if you're looking for a simple option. Home Assistant Companion is the absolute gold standard. It can turn phone sensors into entities in your smart home instantly. Tasker is great for custom logic. For example, if the light level drops below a certain brightness, then send a command to my smart curtains to close.

Using it in the real world

And tips to get started

There are so many real-world use cases for using your phone as a sensor. You can use it as a 3D printer monitor. Use the camera for monitoring, the accelerometer to detect spaghetti vibrations, and the thermal sensors to ensure the room isn't overheating. You can do all of these with just the phone alone, without needing to invest in multiple different sensors, as you would need to with IoT versions.

It can also be the ultimate baby or pet monitor, as it provides you with far better audio and video quality than a $40 Amazon camera. It has the ability to detect loud noises and send you push notifications straight to your phone. It can also be a garage guardian. Use the magnetometer to detect the garage door opening and the light sensor to ensure that you didn't leave the light on in there.

While all of this sounds peachy, there is a slight maintenance catch. The first is battery bloat, which is the biggest risk. Leaving a phone plugged in constantly can damage the battery, causing it to expand and even explode and catch fire. Make sure that you don't do this. The best option is to use a smart plug with an automation to keep the battery somewhere between 20 and 80%, or use a battery idle mod if the phone is rooted.

Subscribe for smart-home guides on repurposing phones

Turn your old phone into a powerful IoT sensor—join the newsletter to receive practical how-tos, safety and thermal management tips, and recommended tools for using phones as smart-home sensors and monitors.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Thermal management is important too. Screens generate heat. Keep the screen off or use kiosk mode apps to keep the device cool. Lastly, ensure that you factory reset the device before starting. It can be easy to just jump straight in without clearing all of your previous data first, but starting with a clean slate can absolutely maximize the CPU resources for the sensor tasks.

Don't let your old phone collect dust

Put it to good use in just a couple of minutes

An old Android phone isn't just good enough for IoT; it's often overqualified and provides you with much better specifications than budget-friendly IoT options. Pull that old OnePlus or Galaxy out of the drawer and set up your first SuperSensor this weekend. By opting for no-code or low-code software to accompany, you can have it set up super quickly and save yourself some extra cash by not having to invest in separate sensors. It also gives your potential e-waste a whole new lease of life.

All of these would need to be purchased separately when picking up IoT components, and the majority of the time, the sensors present in old phones are better quality overall. An ambient light sensor in a phone is highly sensitive and can distinguish between just dim and sunset. Microphones can be used as a glass break detector or a noise floor monitor. A magnetometer can detect if a large metal object, like a car or garage door, is present. The camera on your old smartphone is likely 12MP plus resolution versus the grainy 240p of cheap IOT cameras.

Using your old phone as even just one of these sensors, let alone all of them, provides you with a much better purpose for your old device than just having it sit on a shelf. It can save you from investing in lower-quality IoT sensors, which can cost you a whole lot of money when all the components are added up.

However, there is a software bridge. The hardware itself is useless without a way to get the data out. There is a range of no-code or low-coding tools you can use if you're looking for a simple option. Home Assistant Companion is the absolute gold standard. It can turn phone sensors into entities in your smart home instantly. Tasker is great for custom logic. For example, if the light level drops below a certain brightness, then send a command to my smart curtains to close.

Using it in the real world

And tips to get started

There are so many real-world use cases for using your phone as a sensor. You can use it as a 3D printer monitor. Use the camera for monitoring, the accelerometer to detect spaghetti vibrations, and the thermal sensors to ensure the room isn't overheating. You can do all of these with just the phone alone, without needing to invest in multiple different sensors, as you would need to with IoT versions.

It can also be the ultimate baby or pet monitor, as it provides you with far better audio and video quality than a $40 Amazon camera. It has the ability to detect loud noises and send you push notifications straight to your phone. It can also be a garage guardian. Use the magnetometer to detect the garage door opening and the light sensor to ensure that you didn't leave the light on in there.

While all of this sounds peachy, there is a slight maintenance catch. The first is battery bloat, which is the biggest risk. Leaving a phone plugged in constantly can damage the battery, causing it to expand and even explode and catch fire. Make sure that you don't do this. The best option is to use a smart plug with an automation to keep the battery somewhere between 20 and 80%, or use a battery idle mod if the phone is rooted.

Subscribe for smart-home guides on repurposing phones

Turn your old phone into a powerful IoT sensor—join the newsletter to receive practical how-tos, safety and thermal management tips, and recommended tools for using phones as smart-home sensors and monitors.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Thermal management is important too. Screens generate heat. Keep the screen off or use kiosk mode apps to keep the device cool. Lastly, ensure that you factory reset the device before starting. It can be easy to just jump straight in without clearing all of your previous data first, but starting with a clean slate can absolutely maximize the CPU resources for the sensor tasks.

Don't let your old phone collect dust

Put it to good use in just a couple of minutes

An old Android phone isn't just good enough for IoT; it's often overqualified and provides you with much better specifications than budget-friendly IoT options. Pull that old OnePlus or Galaxy out of the drawer and set up your first SuperSensor this weekend. By opting for no-code or low-code software to accompany, you can have it set up super quickly and save yourself some extra cash by not having to invest in separate sensors. It also gives your potential e-waste a whole new lease of life.

item.title: CachyOS beats Windows 11 in Cyberpunk 2077 and Space Marine 2 in a new gaming benchmark
item.itunes:title: CachyOS beats Windows 11 in Cyberpunk 2077 and Space Marine 2 in a new gaming benchmark
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • CachyOS often outperforms Windows 11 in benchmarks, usually 3-10% higher FPS.
  • CachyOS beats Windows in Cyberpunk 2077 and Space Marine 2 with higher averages and 1% lows.
  • Windows still ties or beats CachyOS in some games like The Division 2 and The First Descendant.

There was a time when gaming on Linux pretty much boiled down to SuperTuxKart, Battle for Wesnoth, and that's about it. However, in the last decade, Linux gaming has gone from strength to strength thanks to efforts from the open-source community. Now, games using Steam's Proton compatibility layer on Linux are rivaling Windows 11's performance, and it doesn't seem the 'best OS for gaming' is keeping hold of its crown for much longer.

A recent benchmark has delved into how several popular titles run on both Windows 11 and CachyOS, a performance-focused Linux distro. While the two put up an excellent fight and looked pretty toe-to-toe across the board, both Cyberpunk 2077 and Space Marine 2 saw some very impressive performance boosts on CachyOS that gamers should take note of.

CachyOS dethrones Arch as the top desktop distro for Linux gamers on ProtonDB

It ended a five year streak.

30
By  Simon Batt

CachyOS takes some major wins versus Windows 11 in two big titles

Cyberpunk 2077 and Space Marine 2 seemed to prefer Linux

As spotted by Notebook Check, the benchmark was performed by NJ Tech on YouTube, who pitted Windows 11 versus CachyOS on the same PC. By the looks of the on-screen stats, the computer was running an AMD Ryzen 5 5600X and a Radeon RX 6700 XT. NJ Tech then posted both the average framerate and the average of the lowest 1% framerate values for each operating system.

The tests showed some very interesting results. In the majority of the tests, CachyOS took the win, scoring around 3-10% better FPS rates than Windows 11. On top of that, there were a handful of very clear victories for CachyOS. First, Space Marine 2 ran a lot smoother on the Linux distro, posting an 81 FPS average over Windows 11's 68 FPS. CachyOS kept things pretty consistent, with a 1% low of 72 FPS versus Windows 11's 58 FPS.

Cyberpunk 2077 was also a solid win for CachyOS, as it achieved a 98 FPS average with a 1% low of 76 FPS. Windows 11 scored a 91 FPS average and a 1% low of 63 FPS, making CachyOS the clear winner for rendering a very graphically-intensive game.

However, it wasn't all good news for CachyOS. Windows 11 managed to tie it in average FPS in The Division 2, and got 4 percentage points higher in the 1% lows. Windows 11 took a more decisive win in The First Descendant, with Windows 11 posting an average of 63 FPS and a 1% low of 39 FPS, versus CachyOS's 54 FPS average and a 1% low of 38 FPS. However, the writing on the wall is pretty clear: Windows is no longer the uncontested champ of gaming.

3 reasons Linux is the best alternative to Windows for gamers

Linux is still the best option for gamers looking to get away from Microsofts grasp

6
By  Elliot Alexander

item.title: Your gaming monitor's refresh rate matters less than these 4 features
item.itunes:title: Your gaming monitor's refresh rate matters less than these 4 features
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I firmly believe that a display upgrade can be more impactful than a new graphics card, especially when you already have a decent GPU. The monitor is one of the most important parts of any gaming setup — it's responsible for delivering your gaming experience to your eyes. Many gamers are obsessed with blazing-fast refresh rates when buying a new monitor, but I think it doesn't matter much once you cross a certain threshold. Even 165–180Hz displays can feel phenomenal if you nail the other aspects that determine gaming immersion. From the size of the screen and the type of panel to the HDR capabilities and motion clarity, a lot of things matter more than the raw refresh rate of your monitor. Before you drop big money on a monitor upgrade, make sure you're buying something that will actually feel like a big upgrade.

5 ways your monitor is the biggest bottleneck in your gaming setup

Upgrade your old monitor before investing in a new GPU

By  Tanveer Singh

Form factor and resolution

The big-ticket items

The first thing you notice about a display is the size and shape. No matter the other specs, a 24" monitor can only feel so immersive. Today, most gaming monitors come in 27" or 32" sizes, either of which is more than enough for enjoying high-end gaming. Besides flat displays, you also have the option to go with a curved ultrawide panel for enhanced immersion. These monitors are meant to cover your field of view, making you feel part of the game. The degree to which they're able to do that depends on the screen size and curve aggressiveness, but overall, curved monitors are a step-up on the immersion ladder. You can choose from ultrawide or super ultrawide displays, but you also need to be prepared for the atypical aspect ratios. Unlike the 16:9 aspect ratio of flat displays, ultrawide and super ultrawide monitors have 21:9 and 32:9 aspect ratios, respectively. Most games support wide aspect ratios, but you could still be forced to see some content with pillarboxing (black bars).

The resolution is deeply tied to the screen size, since both need to increase together if you wish to maintain a sharp image. This is why 1080p displays are only ideal for screens up to 24" in size. 27" monitors look good at 1440p or 4K, and 32" monitors should ideally be used in 4K only. Ultrawide and super ultrawide monitors have different resolutions based on the form factor. If you want a balance between image clarity and GPU performance, 1440p (QHD), WQHD, or UWQHD are decent picks. For the sharpest image, go with a 4K display, but be prepared to spend a premium if you also want a high refresh rate and a high-end panel.

The screen size and resolution together are more important than the refresh rate of your display. Without a large and sharp enough screen, your high-refresh-rate display will not deliver a satisfying gaming experience. A smooth framerate is essential, but once your display is capable of 144–165Hz, going higher won't be as impactful as increasing the screen size and resolution.

Buying a gaming monitor? — Stop making these 7 mistakes

Make sure you're buying the right display for your gaming setup

1
By  Tanveer Singh

Real-world brightness

Let there be light

Almost every monitor on the market gets slapped with an HDR certification, but it means very little in real life. Even if you have an IPS or VA display with a decent contrast ratio, the brightness is where most models fail to deliver. Without sufficient real-world (full-screen, not peak) brightness, you can't expect to get a satisfying HDR experience. The brightness needed to display highlights at sufficient contrast with the darker areas of the screen is just not possible on most budget and even mid-range displays. Brightness levels have been increasing across the board, but we still have a long way to go before the average gaming monitor is truly HDR-ready.

You can find out the real-world brightness of the monitor you're considering from reputable third-party reviews — I prefer Hardware Unboxed and RTINGS. You need at least an HDR600 certification for entry-level HDR, and HDR1000 for true HDR. At the end of the day, real-world testing matters more than the certification, and your monitor's full-screen brightness is a better indicator of HDR quality than the peak brightness numbers. Without an impactful HDR experience in gaming or movies, your monitor won't "feel" high-end, no matter the refresh rate.

We need to talk about the sorry state of HDR on mainstream monitors

Unless you have a fancy OLED monitor, you're probably not experiencing any "HDR" on your PC

By  Tanveer Singh

Motion clarity

You don't want a messy blur on-screen

Your monitor's refresh rate alone doesn't determine a responsive gaming experience. Sure, a higher refresh rate will contribute to smoother motion, but you also need a low response time and proper panel tuning. Many monitors will claim a 1ms response time, but only deliver it under extreme conditions riddled with ghosting and other artifacts. Third-party reviews will often uncover such anomalies present on subpar displays, especially in darker scenes. The panel type plays a crucial role in how low the response time can be — OLED displays are famous for their 0.03ms response times.

Then, there are features like backlight strobing and black frame insertion to look for. These are techniques that increase the motion clarity by turning the backlight off or inserting a black frame, respectively, between each refresh to reduce the perceived motion blur. These are essentially tricks to fool your brain into perceiving higher motion clarity. These techniques have downsides, particularly flickering and the inability to use them alongside adaptive sync. However, with the right tuning, they can positively impact your display's motion clarity.

3 monitor specs that affect motion clarity more than refresh rate

Even a 240Hz monitor can have poor motion clarity

1
By  Hamlin Rozario

Panel type

HDR matters

Finally, the type of panel on your monitor is probably the biggest contributor to your gaming experience. Ever since OLED panels arrived on gaming monitors, the level of immersion and responsiveness possible on gaming displays has gotten a huge upgrade. IPS and VA displays are still great, especially when significantly cheaper than OLEDs, but the self-emitting pixels of OLED panels make them superior to every other panel technology. They aren't perfect in every regard, since burn-in concerns and inferior text clarity issues still exist. However, they have mostly superseded IPS and VA displays in every other department. Even the inferior brightness problem has been solved with 4th-gen tandem OLED displays. And newer subpixel layouts have improved text clarity a lot over the years.

Subscribe for smarter monitor upgrade recommendations

Make future display upgrades worth it - subscribe to the newsletter for clear, research-backed buying guidance on monitors and related hardware. Get practical tests, trade-off analysis, and buying confidence before you spend.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The HDR experience on an OLED display is simply unbeatable. Thanks to the lack of a massive backlight, OLEDs can turn off completely where the screen needs to be dark, offering true blacks. Combined with high brightness levels, this allows OLEDs to deliver strong HDR content in both games and movies. The self-emitting pixels are also way faster than those on other display types, offering response times in the 0.01–0.03m s range. Whether it's the contrast ratio, HDR quality, or motion clarity, it's hard to beat OLED monitors. With 2026 dropping minimum OLED pricing to the $350-$400 range, these monitors are more affordable than ever.

AOC Q27GAZD
$360 $380 Save $20
$360 at Amazon
3 signs OLED monitors will finally be affordable in 2026

The OLED monitor market is about to get exciting

2
By  Tanveer Singh

Refresh rate is important, but it doesn't matter much after a point

Your monitor's refresh rate is a key determinant of its motion responsiveness, but once you've breached the 144–165Hz range, going higher matters less than upgrades in other areas. The panel type is a huge contributor to the image clarity and HDR quality. The form factor and resolution also decide how immersive your monitor will actually feel. And without low response times and high brightness levels, you can't expect the motion clarity or HDR experience to be enjoyable.

Real-world brightness

Let there be light

Almost every monitor on the market gets slapped with an HDR certification, but it means very little in real life. Even if you have an IPS or VA display with a decent contrast ratio, the brightness is where most models fail to deliver. Without sufficient real-world (full-screen, not peak) brightness, you can't expect to get a satisfying HDR experience. The brightness needed to display highlights at sufficient contrast with the darker areas of the screen is just not possible on most budget and even mid-range displays. Brightness levels have been increasing across the board, but we still have a long way to go before the average gaming monitor is truly HDR-ready.

You can find out the real-world brightness of the monitor you're considering from reputable third-party reviews — I prefer Hardware Unboxed and RTINGS. You need at least an HDR600 certification for entry-level HDR, and HDR1000 for true HDR. At the end of the day, real-world testing matters more than the certification, and your monitor's full-screen brightness is a better indicator of HDR quality than the peak brightness numbers. Without an impactful HDR experience in gaming or movies, your monitor won't "feel" high-end, no matter the refresh rate.

We need to talk about the sorry state of HDR on mainstream monitors

Unless you have a fancy OLED monitor, you're probably not experiencing any "HDR" on your PC

By  Tanveer Singh

Motion clarity

You don't want a messy blur on-screen

Your monitor's refresh rate alone doesn't determine a responsive gaming experience. Sure, a higher refresh rate will contribute to smoother motion, but you also need a low response time and proper panel tuning. Many monitors will claim a 1ms response time, but only deliver it under extreme conditions riddled with ghosting and other artifacts. Third-party reviews will often uncover such anomalies present on subpar displays, especially in darker scenes. The panel type plays a crucial role in how low the response time can be — OLED displays are famous for their 0.03ms response times.

Then, there are features like backlight strobing and black frame insertion to look for. These are techniques that increase the motion clarity by turning the backlight off or inserting a black frame, respectively, between each refresh to reduce the perceived motion blur. These are essentially tricks to fool your brain into perceiving higher motion clarity. These techniques have downsides, particularly flickering and the inability to use them alongside adaptive sync. However, with the right tuning, they can positively impact your display's motion clarity.

3 monitor specs that affect motion clarity more than refresh rate

Even a 240Hz monitor can have poor motion clarity

1
By  Hamlin Rozario

Panel type

HDR matters

Finally, the type of panel on your monitor is probably the biggest contributor to your gaming experience. Ever since OLED panels arrived on gaming monitors, the level of immersion and responsiveness possible on gaming displays has gotten a huge upgrade. IPS and VA displays are still great, especially when significantly cheaper than OLEDs, but the self-emitting pixels of OLED panels make them superior to every other panel technology. They aren't perfect in every regard, since burn-in concerns and inferior text clarity issues still exist. However, they have mostly superseded IPS and VA displays in every other department. Even the inferior brightness problem has been solved with 4th-gen tandem OLED displays. And newer subpixel layouts have improved text clarity a lot over the years.

Subscribe for smarter monitor upgrade recommendations

Make future display upgrades worth it - subscribe to the newsletter for clear, research-backed buying guidance on monitors and related hardware. Get practical tests, trade-off analysis, and buying confidence before you spend.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The HDR experience on an OLED display is simply unbeatable. Thanks to the lack of a massive backlight, OLEDs can turn off completely where the screen needs to be dark, offering true blacks. Combined with high brightness levels, this allows OLEDs to deliver strong HDR content in both games and movies. The self-emitting pixels are also way faster than those on other display types, offering response times in the 0.01–0.03m s range. Whether it's the contrast ratio, HDR quality, or motion clarity, it's hard to beat OLED monitors. With 2026 dropping minimum OLED pricing to the $350-$400 range, these monitors are more affordable than ever.

AOC Q27GAZD
$360 $380 Save $20
$360 at Amazon
3 signs OLED monitors will finally be affordable in 2026

The OLED monitor market is about to get exciting

2
By  Tanveer Singh

Refresh rate is important, but it doesn't matter much after a point

Your monitor's refresh rate is a key determinant of its motion responsiveness, but once you've breached the 144–165Hz range, going higher matters less than upgrades in other areas. The panel type is a huge contributor to the image clarity and HDR quality. The form factor and resolution also decide how immersive your monitor will actually feel. And without low response times and high brightness levels, you can't expect the motion clarity or HDR experience to be enjoyable.

item.title: I tried Replit, Lovable, and Claude Code to build the same app — here's the only one worth using
item.itunes:title: I tried Replit, Lovable, and Claude Code to build the same app — here's the only one worth using
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Regardless of what you do, I'm sure at least one app idea has crossed your mind over the years. Perhaps a random frustration you faced at 2 AM and thought "how is there still not an app for this" and scribbled it into your Notes app. Or you saw someone else's app blow up and quietly muttered "I had that idea months ago." Maybe you have a running list of random app ideas somewhere — ideas you swore you'd build when you have the time to learn how to actually code. Well, in 2026, you don't really need to go the traditional route anymore. You don't need to commit hours to learning how to code or paying a handsome amount of money to an app developer to build their version of your vision.

Hundreds of AI-powered app builders now exist that claim all you need to do is describe your idea in plain English, and the AI will go ahead and build it for you. Three of the biggest names leading this space right now are Replit, Lovable, and of course, Claude Code. Now, if you've been wanting to finally bring your app ideas to life, you've likely seen people swearing by each of these tools. So, to see which one actually delivers the best results, I decided to put them to the test by using each to build the exact same app. Here's how it went...

I used the same exact prompt across all three tools

But I kept it intentionally vague

When I'm vibe-coding, I've found that the best way to get results that match my exact vision is having incredibly detailed prompts. I don't necessarily mean longer prompts, but I mean a value-rich prompt that clearly communicates what you want. The more context you give, the less the AI has to guess and the closer the output lands to what's in your head. But for this test, I deliberately went the other way.

I kept the prompt a bit vague on purpose. If my prompt covered every detail down to the exact colors, the specific libraries, the precise layout of every page, then I'm doing most of the "thinking" part for the tool and any halfway decent AI would produce roughly the same result. I wanted to more-so see how each tool interprets a rough idea I have, and then what it whips up. How functional is the app it creates on the very first go? Do the features work as I envisioned? Is it a solid enough first prototype to genuinely capture what I had in mind?

While a lot of the answers to these questions come down to the exact prompt I'm using, the average user who wants to build an app isn't going to go through all that hassle. They're going to describe their idea the way they'd explain it to a friend. So, that's exactly how I wrote the prompt.

The idea I had was an app where you upload your bank statement as a CSV and the app roasts your spending habits, categorizes your transactions, gives you a letter grade, and generates a brutally funny AI-powered breakdown of how you spend your money! It also lets you track your letter grade over time. So, while the idea was more or less an AI wrapper, it still touches enough real complexity to be a fair test! In my prompt, I mentioned the core features I wanted but I left out things like specific design direction and what tech stack to use.

Claude Code is the only one that passed the real-world test

Isn't that what really matters?

Let's begin with the tool that impressed me the most in this test, and the one I think is genuinely worth using for something like this (even if you're a beginner): Claude Code. All three of the tools handled the sample data I had asked for just fine, which isn't surprising at all. The sample data is quite literally fake data that the tool itself generated, so of course it knows how to parse it! The real test was what happens when you upload a bank statement as a CSV file! That's real-world data in a format that the tool isn't accustomed to, and it's what helps determine whether the app actually works or just looks like it does.

I exported my own bank statement as a CSV and uploaded it to all three, and Claude Code was the only one that handled it properly. It did get the currency wrong, but that's more-so something I should have specified in the prompt! For Replit and Lovable, I had to go in and follow up with additional prompts just to get them to parse the CSV correctly. On the other hand, Claude's version handled it perfectly on the first go! Claude's version of the roast was also the best out of the three!

I cancelled my ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini subscriptions for Claude — and I should have sooner

Wish I did this sooner.

51
By  Mahnoor Faisal

The output was also the only version to have a neat graph of daily spending, and had an extremely interactive interface that was nice to look at! That said, I do think the UI looked very generic and you could tell it was vibe-coded. It had that classic look that every vibe-coded app ends up with these days, and I think this is something Replit and Lovable handled better. Claude Code was the tool that took the longest to complete building this app out, but given that the end result was the most functional and the most complete of the three, I'd say the extra time was well worth it.

Replit's output looked the best

But it didn't actually work

Honestly, when Replit's version first loaded, I thought it was going to win. The UI was the best out of the three. For example, it had this Mac-style window frame around the roast section, complete with the little traffic light dots at the top. The roast would appear as if it was being typed out in real-time, like a typewriter effect, which was a really nice touch and made the whole experience feel more dramatic and fun. Ironically enough, despite the roast section being my favorite design element of the app, it was also the most broken part.

Instead of actually displaying the roast as readable text, it dumped the raw API response straight onto the screen! All it took was one more prompt to fix it, but that's exactly what I was trying to put to the test here! When youre comparing tools based on what they produce from a single prompt, details like this certainly matter. Like I mentioned above, the file upload also didn't work with my actual statements on the first try. Again, fixable with follow-up prompts, but that's two core features that needed extra work.

Lovable was the fastest to build

But the output was just okay

Lovable was the first tool to finish building, and the app it produced had a working roast feature. The UI of the tool was definitely clean, but it didn't really stand out. It did sort of give the vibe-coded look I was talking about earlier, and just looked like every other AI-generated app you've seen. The roast itself actually worked, so it's clear that Lovable handled the AI integration better than Replit on the first go. However, just like Replit, it couldn't parse a real CSV file initially.

Subscribe for hands-on AI app-builder breakdowns

Want confident, hands-on guidance on AI app builders? Subscribe to the newsletter for practical comparisons, honest analyses, and clear verdicts on Claude Code, Replit, Lovable and other build-with-AI platforms to help you pick the right tool.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

So essentially, Lovable didn't have Replit's design edge or Claude Code's functionality and essentially sat somewhere between the two. It's definitely the tool I'd turn to when I need a quick working prototype given how fast it was to finish, but if I'm building something I actually want to use or show to people, I'd rather wait the extra time and go with either Claude or Replit.

To be fair, Replit and Lovable are free to try

Now, while you can try Claude out for completely free, Claude Code (which I used for this test) requires a paid subscription. So if you're just exploring and want to test the waters without spending anything, Replit and Lovable both have free tiers that'll let you build something like this without paying anything. But if you've got an idea you're actually serious above, Claude Code is the one I'd recommend!

item.title: Most people use NotebookLM like a chatbot, and that's why they miss what it's actually good at
item.itunes:title: Most people use NotebookLM like a chatbot, and that's why they miss what it's actually good at
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I watched someone paste a 10,000-word report into NotebookLM, ask it to summarize the main points, and then close the tab. They got their answer, sure. But they also completely missed the point.

NotebookLM isn't ChatGPT with a file upload button. It's Google's document-grounded research tool that generates insights by cross-referencing your sources, building structured briefing docs, and creating Audio Overviews that synthesize multiple documents into conversational explanations. I didn't realize what I was missing either until I stopped treating it like a better Ctrl + F.

Why the chatbot mental model breaks NotebookLM

It's built for synthesis, not interrogation

When you upload a PDF to ChatGPT or Claude, the AI reads it and answers questions. That's useful, but it's fundamentally reactive. You ask, it responds, and the relationship usually ends there.

NotebookLM is designed around sources as the organizing principle. You upload multiple documents, and the tool generates the option to funnel the information into FAQs, study guides, and briefing documents that pull from all of them. It's not waiting for you to ask the right question; it's already trying to show you connections.

These features do something most people overlook: they anticipate questions based on what's actually in your sources, not what a language model thinks you might want to know. Perplexity and ChatGPT treat documents as context for answers. NotebookLM treats them as a corpus to be structured and cross-referenced. That difference matters when you're doing real research instead of just extracting facts.

Until NotebookLM, I never believed AI could be this game-changing for productivity

It transformed my view of AI, for the better.

7
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Audio Overviews turn reading into listening

Two AI hosts discuss your sources like a podcast

Here's the feature that sounds gimmicky until you actually use it: Audio Overviews. You click a button, and NotebookLM generates a podcast-style conversation between two AI hosts who discuss your uploaded sources. They summarize, debate, and contextualize it.

I tested this with a mix of papers and essays on knowledge management and a few blog posts about PKM tools. The resulting 8-minute audio had the hosts unpacking the difference between "information retrieval" and "knowledge synthesis," citing specific papers, and making connections I hadn't noticed myself.

This isn't anything like text-to-speech. The hosts use conversational filler ("Right, and building on that..."), disagree with each other occasionally, and structure the discussion with an intro, body, and conclusion. It's weirdly effective for processing dense material while doing something else like commuting, cooking, or walking.

The Notebook is an actual structured output

It builds artifacts you can actually use

When you ask NotebookLM a question, it doesn't just answer in the chat. It can save responses directly into the Notebook panel, where you're building a structured document that persists across your research session.

This is where the "chatbot" comparison falls apart completely. In ChatGPT or Claude, your conversation history is a linear thread. In NotebookLM, you're actively constructing a reference document while you work. Ask about conflicting viewpoints in your sources, and you can save that comparison table to your Notebook. Request a timeline of events, and it gets added as a section you can edit and export.

Saved notes stay available across sessions, unlike scrolling chat history, and support organization, exports to Google Docs, and collaboration. They're ideal for research, keeping key insights like tables or summaries handy without losing them on refresh.

Source citations make it verifiable

Every claim links back to the document

One of NotebookLM's most underrated features: inline citations. Every answer includes footnote-style references that link directly to the passage in your uploaded source where the information is found. Click a citation, and it jumps you to the exact paragraph in the PDF or doc. This is critical for research workflows where you need to verify claims, pull direct quotes, or trace an argument back to its origin.

ChatGPT with file uploads doesn't do this reliably, often showing the wrong page numbers. Claude's citation system works, but it isn't as tightly integrated with source navigation. Perplexity cites web sources, but if you're working with proprietary documents or offline research, that doesn't help.

Get NotebookLM insights — subscribe to the newsletter

Subscribe to the newsletter for focused coverage of NotebookLM and other document-grounded research tools, plus deeper analysis, practical examples, and case studies that help translate ideas into usable research workflows.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

NotebookLM's won't make claims it can't trace to your sources. That makes it less creative than ChatGPT for brainstorming, but far more trustworthy for synthesis work where accuracy matters more than novelty.

When the chatbot instinct actually limits you

Don’t ignore the Notebook builder

Most people open NotebookLM, ask a question, get an answer, and leave. That's fine for quick lookups, but you're ignoring cross-source FAQ generation, Audio Overviews, and structured briefing docs.

The tool is designed for iterative research, not one-off queries. It's for people writing literature reviews, synthesizing market research, preparing for deep work sessions, or building knowledge bases from scattered sources. Start treating NotebookLM like a workspace instead of a chatbot, and it actually becomes useful.

NotebookLM

NotebookLM is Google’s AI-powered research assistant that turns your uploaded documents, notes, and sources into an intelligent, conversational workspace that helps you connect ideas, summarize insights, and generate new ones.

See at NotebookLM
item.description:
I watched someone paste a 10,000-word report into NotebookLM, ask it to summarize the main points, and then close the tab. They got their answer, sure. But they also completely missed the point.

NotebookLM isn't ChatGPT with a file upload button. It's Google's document-grounded research tool that generates insights by cross-referencing your sources, building structured briefing docs, and creating Audio Overviews that synthesize multiple documents into conversational explanations. I didn't realize what I was missing either until I stopped treating it like a better Ctrl + F.

Why the chatbot mental model breaks NotebookLM

It's built for synthesis, not interrogation

When you upload a PDF to ChatGPT or Claude, the AI reads it and answers questions. That's useful, but it's fundamentally reactive. You ask, it responds, and the relationship usually ends there.

NotebookLM is designed around sources as the organizing principle. You upload multiple documents, and the tool generates the option to funnel the information into FAQs, study guides, and briefing documents that pull from all of them. It's not waiting for you to ask the right question; it's already trying to show you connections.

These features do something most people overlook: they anticipate questions based on what's actually in your sources, not what a language model thinks you might want to know. Perplexity and ChatGPT treat documents as context for answers. NotebookLM treats them as a corpus to be structured and cross-referenced. That difference matters when you're doing real research instead of just extracting facts.

Until NotebookLM, I never believed AI could be this game-changing for productivity

It transformed my view of AI, for the better.

7
By  Mahnoor Faisal

Audio Overviews turn reading into listening

Two AI hosts discuss your sources like a podcast

Here's the feature that sounds gimmicky until you actually use it: Audio Overviews. You click a button, and NotebookLM generates a podcast-style conversation between two AI hosts who discuss your uploaded sources. They summarize, debate, and contextualize it.

I tested this with a mix of papers and essays on knowledge management and a few blog posts about PKM tools. The resulting 8-minute audio had the hosts unpacking the difference between "information retrieval" and "knowledge synthesis," citing specific papers, and making connections I hadn't noticed myself.

This isn't anything like text-to-speech. The hosts use conversational filler ("Right, and building on that..."), disagree with each other occasionally, and structure the discussion with an intro, body, and conclusion. It's weirdly effective for processing dense material while doing something else like commuting, cooking, or walking.

The Notebook is an actual structured output

It builds artifacts you can actually use

When you ask NotebookLM a question, it doesn't just answer in the chat. It can save responses directly into the Notebook panel, where you're building a structured document that persists across your research session.

This is where the "chatbot" comparison falls apart completely. In ChatGPT or Claude, your conversation history is a linear thread. In NotebookLM, you're actively constructing a reference document while you work. Ask about conflicting viewpoints in your sources, and you can save that comparison table to your Notebook. Request a timeline of events, and it gets added as a section you can edit and export.

Saved notes stay available across sessions, unlike scrolling chat history, and support organization, exports to Google Docs, and collaboration. They're ideal for research, keeping key insights like tables or summaries handy without losing them on refresh.

Source citations make it verifiable

Every claim links back to the document

One of NotebookLM's most underrated features: inline citations. Every answer includes footnote-style references that link directly to the passage in your uploaded source where the information is found. Click a citation, and it jumps you to the exact paragraph in the PDF or doc. This is critical for research workflows where you need to verify claims, pull direct quotes, or trace an argument back to its origin.

ChatGPT with file uploads doesn't do this reliably, often showing the wrong page numbers. Claude's citation system works, but it isn't as tightly integrated with source navigation. Perplexity cites web sources, but if you're working with proprietary documents or offline research, that doesn't help.

Get NotebookLM insights — subscribe to the newsletter

Subscribe to the newsletter for focused coverage of NotebookLM and other document-grounded research tools, plus deeper analysis, practical examples, and case studies that help translate ideas into usable research workflows.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

NotebookLM's won't make claims it can't trace to your sources. That makes it less creative than ChatGPT for brainstorming, but far more trustworthy for synthesis work where accuracy matters more than novelty.

When the chatbot instinct actually limits you

Don’t ignore the Notebook builder

Most people open NotebookLM, ask a question, get an answer, and leave. That's fine for quick lookups, but you're ignoring cross-source FAQ generation, Audio Overviews, and structured briefing docs.

The tool is designed for iterative research, not one-off queries. It's for people writing literature reviews, synthesizing market research, preparing for deep work sessions, or building knowledge bases from scattered sources. Start treating NotebookLM like a workspace instead of a chatbot, and it actually becomes useful.

NotebookLM

NotebookLM is Google’s AI-powered research assistant that turns your uploaded documents, notes, and sources into an intelligent, conversational workspace that helps you connect ideas, summarize insights, and generate new ones.

See at NotebookLM

item.title: CachyOS has a one-click Windows VM button, and dual-booting feels completely outdated
item.itunes:title: CachyOS has a one-click Windows VM button, and dual-booting feels completely outdated
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For years, dual-booting felt like the grown-up answer for anyone who wanted Linux without fully giving up Windows. You got native performance, direct hardware access, and the comfort of knowing your fallback OS was sitting right there, waiting for you at startup. That made sense when Linux still had wider compatibility gaps and when setting up a virtual machine felt like a side quest rather than a practical solution. But the moment an operating system starts treating a Windows VM as an everyday tool rather than an enthusiast project, that old logic starts to wobble.

CachyOS quietly acknowledges quietly acknowledges that most of us don’t need Windows all the time, we just need it sometimes.

That’s exactly why CachyOS feels so interesting right now. It doesn’t just make Linux fast or polished, though it clearly cares about both. It also quietly acknowledges a truth a lot of desktop users have already arrived at on their own: most of us don’t need Windows all the time, we just need it sometimes. When a distro gives you a one-click path to spin up a Windows VM using Winboat, dual-booting starts to feel less like flexibility and more like leftover baggage.

4 reasons you should embrace virtual machines instead of jumping straight to dual-booting

Give VMs a try

6
By  Anurag Singh

CachyOS makes occasional Windows use feel refreshingly painless

A built-in shortcut changes the whole mental equation

The biggest thing dual-booting used to offer was convenience, even if it was a clunky kind of convenience. You rebooted, picked the other OS, and got on with your task. That process was never elegant, but it was predictable, and for a long time, it felt easier than configuring virtualization. CachyOS flips that trade-off in a way that’s hard to ignore, because the barrier to entry suddenly looks much lower. In the latest versions of CachyOS Hello, you can now install Winboat from the Apps/Tweaks menu.

That matters more than Linux veterans sometimes admit. Plenty of people are willing to use Linux as their main desktop now, but they still have one or two Windows-only needs hanging around like unpaid parking tickets. Maybe it’s a stubborn launcher, a niche utility, or some oddball hardware updater that only exists for Windows. If launching a Winboat WindowsVM is simple enough that it doesn’t feel like setting up lab equipment, the whole idea of maintaining a second bare-metal install starts to lose its shine.

CachyOS makes installing Virtual Machine Manager almost as easy, but WinBoat is the route to follow if you only need occasional access to a Windows installation.

There’s also something psychologically important about not having to leave your main environment. Rebooting into Windows breaks your flow, even when the reboot only takes a minute or two. Winboat keeps Linux at the center and turns Windows into a tool instead of a destination. That’s a subtle shift, but it changes the relationship completely, and it’s one reason dual-booting suddenly feels like an older answer to a newer problem.

Keeping Linux as the host is simply a better deal

Your main desktop should stay where your work lives

Once Linux becomes the host operating system and Windows the guest, priorities become much clearer. Your files stay where you expect them, your browser session stays open, your notifications stay in place, and your whole desktop doesn’t vanish just because one application has platform baggage. That’s a much more practical setup than splitting your life between two full installations. Instead of treating both operating systems like equal citizens, you’re finally being honest about which one you actually live in.

That honesty is what makes the Winboat route feel so modern. A lot of people who dual-boot aren’t really using two operating systems equally, even if they like telling themselves they are. They spend most of their time in one OS and drop into the other for a narrow list of tasks. When that’s the reality, dedicating a whole partition, an update cycle, a bootloader dance, and a chunk of mental energy to the secondary system starts to feel excessive.

CachyOS is tapping into that reality rather than fighting it. If your primary desktop is Linux because you prefer the performance, package management, customization, or overall feel, then Windows doesn’t need to sit on the same throne. It can sit in a box, do its little job, and close when you’re done. That may sound dismissive, but for many users, that’s exactly the right role for it.

There are still plenty of reasons dual-booting refuses to die

Some tasks still demand direct hardware without compromise

Dual-booting isn’t outdated for everyone, and pretending otherwise would flatten a very real distinction. There are still workloads where native Windows access matters a lot, especially if you care about top-tier gaming compatibility, anti-cheat behavior, GPU passthrough complexity, or specialized software that gets grumpy inside a VM. In those cases, the old approach remains brutally effective. You boot into Windows, use the hardware directly, and remove a whole category of potential headaches.

Resource constraints can make the VM story less appealing, too. Not every machine has enough RAM, storage, or CPU headroom to host Linux comfortably while also running a decent Windows virtual machine. On a higher-end desktop or laptop, that overhead may feel small enough to shrug off. On more modest hardware, it can feel like asking your computer to juggle while climbing stairs. That’s when dual-booting starts to look less old-fashioned and more sensible.

There’s also the matter of familiarity. Dual-booting may be annoying, but it’s easy to understand. A virtual machine introduces more moving parts, more terminology, and more chances for someone to hit a weird edge case and decide the whole experiment wasn’t worth it. For users who just want a guaranteed path to a Windows session now and then, rebooting may still feel more dependable than learning a new workflow.

Even with those limits, the old compromise feels less necessary

Most users do not need the old escape hatch

The important thing, though, is that the existence of edge cases doesn’t preserve dual-booting as the default answer. It just means there are still exceptions, and exceptions aren’t the same thing as best practice. Most people who are curious about Linux on the desktop aren’t chasing benchmark trophies or trying to squeeze the last watt of performance out of a demanding workload. They’re browsing, writing, editing, tinkering, gaming selectively, and occasionally needing one stubborn Windows app that hasn’t moved on.

For those users, a VM is often a better expression of reality than a second install. It says Windows is useful, not essential. It says Linux is home, and Windows is visiting with a purpose. That’s a more stable arrangement than dual-booting, where every reboot can turn into a little identity crisis about which operating system you’re really committed to using.

And that’s where CachyOS earns the stronger claim. A one-click Windows VM button doesn’t magically erase every technical limitation, but it makes the better workflow more visible and more approachable. Instead of asking people to become virtualization hobbyists before they can trust Linux full-time, it lowers the friction and lets the idea prove itself. Once that happens, dual-booting starts to feel less like freedom and more like a leftover habit from when Linux had fewer practical answers.

Why this changes the Linux desktop conversation

What makes this feel bigger than a neat distro feature is the message underneath it. CachyOS isn’t saying Windows compatibility no longer matters; it clearly still does. It’s saying Linux users shouldn’t have to choose between full commitment and constant compromise. That’s a much healthier place for desktop Linux to be, and it makes the platform easier to recommend to people who still have one foot in Microsoft’s world.

If you’re wondering whether CachyOS can really replace a traditional dual-boot setup, the short answer is yes, at least for plenty of people. When Linux handles your everyday workload and Windows sits in a VM for the rare task that still needs it, the whole arrangement starts to feel a lot more practical.

  1. CachyOS lets Linux stay at the center of your daily computing, so your files, apps, browser sessions, and overall workflow all remain in one place.
  2. Windows becomes something you open only when necessary, which makes it feel more like a utility than a second digital life you have to maintain.
  3. That setup also cuts down on dual-boot headaches, since you’re no longer dealing with partitions, bootloader issues, or two full operating systems competing for attention.
  4. A one-click Windows VM lowers the risk of going all-in on Linux, because one stubborn Windows-only task no longer feels like a reason to keep a full separate installation around.

Dual-booting won’t disappear tomorrow, and it doesn’t need to. But when a distro starts making Windows access this easy without asking you to surrender your main desktop, the old setup stops feeling like the smart middle ground it once was. It feels heavier, slower, and oddly ceremonial for something most people only need in short bursts. That’s why CachyOS stands out here, because it makes Linux feel like the default and Windows feel like the backup plan, which is probably how it should’ve been all along.

CachyOS

CachyOS is an Arch distribution that makes running Windows VMs a trivial, one-click trick.

See at CachyOS

That’s a much more practical setup than splitting your life between two full installations. Instead of treating both operating systems like equal citizens, you’re finally being honest about which one you actually live in.

That honesty is what makes the Winboat route feel so modern. A lot of people who dual-boot aren’t really using two operating systems equally, even if they like telling themselves they are. They spend most of their time in one OS and drop into the other for a narrow list of tasks. When that’s the reality, dedicating a whole partition, an update cycle, a bootloader dance, and a chunk of mental energy to the secondary system starts to feel excessive.

CachyOS is tapping into that reality rather than fighting it. If your primary desktop is Linux because you prefer the performance, package management, customization, or overall feel, then Windows doesn’t need to sit on the same throne. It can sit in a box, do its little job, and close when you’re done. That may sound dismissive, but for many users, that’s exactly the right role for it.

There are still plenty of reasons dual-booting refuses to die

Some tasks still demand direct hardware without compromise

Dual-booting isn’t outdated for everyone, and pretending otherwise would flatten a very real distinction. There are still workloads where native Windows access matters a lot, especially if you care about top-tier gaming compatibility, anti-cheat behavior, GPU passthrough complexity, or specialized software that gets grumpy inside a VM. In those cases, the old approach remains brutally effective. You boot into Windows, use the hardware directly, and remove a whole category of potential headaches.

Resource constraints can make the VM story less appealing, too. Not every machine has enough RAM, storage, or CPU headroom to host Linux comfortably while also running a decent Windows virtual machine. On a higher-end desktop or laptop, that overhead may feel small enough to shrug off. On more modest hardware, it can feel like asking your computer to juggle while climbing stairs. That’s when dual-booting starts to look less old-fashioned and more sensible.

There’s also the matter of familiarity. Dual-booting may be annoying, but it’s easy to understand. A virtual machine introduces more moving parts, more terminology, and more chances for someone to hit a weird edge case and decide the whole experiment wasn’t worth it. For users who just want a guaranteed path to a Windows session now and then, rebooting may still feel more dependable than learning a new workflow.

Even with those limits, the old compromise feels less necessary

Most users do not need the old escape hatch

The important thing, though, is that the existence of edge cases doesn’t preserve dual-booting as the default answer. It just means there are still exceptions, and exceptions aren’t the same thing as best practice. Most people who are curious about Linux on the desktop aren’t chasing benchmark trophies or trying to squeeze the last watt of performance out of a demanding workload. They’re browsing, writing, editing, tinkering, gaming selectively, and occasionally needing one stubborn Windows app that hasn’t moved on.

For those users, a VM is often a better expression of reality than a second install. It says Windows is useful, not essential. It says Linux is home, and Windows is visiting with a purpose. That’s a more stable arrangement than dual-booting, where every reboot can turn into a little identity crisis about which operating system you’re really committed to using.

And that’s where CachyOS earns the stronger claim. A one-click Windows VM button doesn’t magically erase every technical limitation, but it makes the better workflow more visible and more approachable. Instead of asking people to become virtualization hobbyists before they can trust Linux full-time, it lowers the friction and lets the idea prove itself. Once that happens, dual-booting starts to feel less like freedom and more like a leftover habit from when Linux had fewer practical answers.

Why this changes the Linux desktop conversation

What makes this feel bigger than a neat distro feature is the message underneath it. CachyOS isn’t saying Windows compatibility no longer matters; it clearly still does. It’s saying Linux users shouldn’t have to choose between full commitment and constant compromise. That’s a much healthier place for desktop Linux to be, and it makes the platform easier to recommend to people who still have one foot in Microsoft’s world.

If you’re wondering whether CachyOS can really replace a traditional dual-boot setup, the short answer is yes, at least for plenty of people. When Linux handles your everyday workload and Windows sits in a VM for the rare task that still needs it, the whole arrangement starts to feel a lot more practical.

  1. CachyOS lets Linux stay at the center of your daily computing, so your files, apps, browser sessions, and overall workflow all remain in one place.
  2. Windows becomes something you open only when necessary, which makes it feel more like a utility than a second digital life you have to maintain.
  3. That setup also cuts down on dual-boot headaches, since you’re no longer dealing with partitions, bootloader issues, or two full operating systems competing for attention.
  4. A one-click Windows VM lowers the risk of going all-in on Linux, because one stubborn Windows-only task no longer feels like a reason to keep a full separate installation around.

Dual-booting won’t disappear tomorrow, and it doesn’t need to. But when a distro starts making Windows access this easy without asking you to surrender your main desktop, the old setup stops feeling like the smart middle ground it once was. It feels heavier, slower, and oddly ceremonial for something most people only need in short bursts. That’s why CachyOS stands out here, because it makes Linux feel like the default and Windows feel like the backup plan, which is probably how it should’ve been all along.

CachyOS

CachyOS is an Arch distribution that makes running Windows VMs a trivial, one-click trick.

See at CachyOS

item.title: Google Chrome is flagging YouTube downloader tool yt-dlp as "suspicious"
item.itunes:title: Google Chrome is flagging YouTube downloader tool yt-dlp as "suspicious"
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Chrome flags yt-dlp downloads as "suspicious" and sometimes blocks them with enhanced protection prompts.
  • Some suspect that Google is deliberately blocking yt-dlp to prevent YouTube downloads, but the evidence isn't conclusive.
  • Firefox and antivirus tools also flag yt-dlp; PyInstaller-built binaries often trigger false positives.

In an ideal world for Google, people who want to download YouTube videos for offline viewing would subscribe to YouTube Premium to access that feature. Unfortunately for the tech giant, there are several tools people can use to download YouTube videos without a Premium subscription and bypass the advertisements that would otherwise be served when watched online.

yt-dlp, a command-line audio and video downloader for YouTube, is one of those utilities. According to Hacker News, some Chrome users are beginning to notice that attempts to download yt-dlp in the browser are being blocked and marked as "suspicious," but the truth might be a little less malicious.

4 ways YouTube Premium quietly offers the best value in streaming

Ad-free viewing is just the start

2
By  Marshall Gunnell

It's unclear why the browser is labeling yt-dlp downloads like this

Don't reach for your pitchfork yet

Credit: Google / XDA

I tried downloading yt-dlp, and not only was I warned about the app, but Chrome displayed a second pop-up asking me to enable enhanced protection after it "just blocked a dangerous file from downloading."

For some, this move rings alarm bells. Google ideally doesn't want people downloading YouTube videos onto their devices, and as such, tools like yt-dlp are against its best interests. The theory is that Google has marked yt-dlp as suspicious in an effort to prevent people from downloading the app and using it to download YouTube videos.

However, there's a very real chance that yt-dlp was just marked as a false positive. Posters in the Hacker News thread noted that Firefox also treated the file with suspicion because "this file is not commonly downloaded." Likewise, others point out that the binaries used by yt-dlp are compiled by PyInstaller, which often trigger false positives in antivirus software. I wasn't able to reproduce the same warning in Firefox, despite some users in the Hacker News thread reporting they also encountered the issue with the browser.

It's impossible to say for sure whether this is Google attempting to deliberately block the app or if this is just a false positive. Personally, given that Firefox is reportedly showing similar warnings, I'm leaning toward the latter. There's a good chance something about yt-dlp's files or download count is triggering a "better safe than sorry" mechanism that blocks the download. XDA has reached out to Google for comment.

Have you run into this issue with yt-dlp and Chrome? Let us know in the comments below.

Google finally fixes the most annoying part of watching YouTube while multitasking

Removing a few clicks from the process.

By  Simon Batt
